canons.title: HR-1: No Emojis
canons.rationale: All iconography uses the zi() SVG icon system. No emoji characters in application-rendered UI. Emoji in user-supplied text is passthrough only.

canons.title: HR-2: No Inline Styles
canons.rationale: All styling through CSS classes and CSS custom properties. Never style= attributes. Ensures consistency and theme-ability.

canons.title: HR-3: No Inline Event Handlers
canons.rationale: All interactivity via data-action delegation. Never onclick= attributes. Single delegation root ensures consistent behavior and easier debugging.

canons.title: HR-4: escHtml at All Render Boundaries
canons.rationale: All user-supplied text passes through escHtml() before innerHTML. Prevents XSS and ensures safe rendering of arbitrary input.

canons.title: HR-5: All Spacing/Font/Radius via CSS Tokens
canons.rationale: Use --sp-*, --fs-*, --r-* tokens exclusively. No magic numbers. Ensures design consistency and enables theme/text-size scaling.

canons.title: HR-6: data-action Delegation Universal
canons.rationale: Every interactive element uses data-action attributes. Single event delegation root. Naming convention: verb-noun or domain-verb-noun.

canons.title: HR-7: zi() Returns SVG HTML, Set via innerHTML
canons.rationale: zi() returns SVG HTML strings. Always set via .innerHTML, never .textContent. Ensures icons render as SVG elements, not escaped text.

canons.title: HR-8: Stub Features Show Coming Soon Toast
canons.rationale: Incomplete or stub features show 'Coming soon' via showQLToast(). Never leave dead buttons or silent no-ops.

canons.title: HR-9: Post-Build Multi-Round QA Audit
canons.rationale: After every build session: multi-round QA continuing until only cosmetic bugs remain. List cosmetic bugs at end. Check all HRs, functional flows, null guards, class coverage, overlay close, escHtml/escAttr.

canons.title: HR-10: No text-overflow Ellipsis
canons.rationale: Truncated text hides meaning. Wrap, resize, or restructure — never truncate with ellipsis.

canons.title: HR-11: Math.floor for Currency
canons.rationale: All currency values use Math.floor(). Never round up on money — always in the user's favor.

canons.title: HR-12: Timezone-Safe Dates via localDateStr()
canons.rationale: All date operations use localDateStr() to avoid timezone offset bugs. Never raw new Date().toISOString().slice(0,10).

canons.title: No text-overflow Ellipsis (Global)
canons.rationale: Promoted from SproutLab HR-10 to global canon. Applies across all projects. Truncation hides meaning — always find a layout solution.

canons.title: escHtml at All Render Boundaries (Global)
canons.rationale: All user-supplied text sanitized before innerHTML in every project. Non-negotiable security practice.

canons.title: data-action Delegation (Global)
canons.rationale: Event delegation via data-action attributes across all projects. Never inline onclick handlers.

canons.title: Defensive Depth
canons.rationale: Null guards, fallback defaults, and graceful degradation at every layer. Never assume upstream data is clean. Check before access, fallback before crash.

canons.title: Exhaustive Pass 4 (Bugs Today)
canons.rationale: In the 8-pass SPEC_ITERATION_PROCESS, Pass 4 (Bugs Today) must be exhaustive — enumerate every edge case, null state, overflow, and boundary. Never skim.

canons.title: Shelf Definitions
canons.rationale: Active = currently being built or maintained. Paused = intentionally on hold, will resume. Archived = complete, read-only reference. Abandoned = stopped, no intent to resume.

canons.title: Token Naming Convention
canons.rationale: CSS tokens: --{prefix}-{category}-{variant}. SproutLab: --sp-*, Codex: --cx-*. Spacing: --sp-N. Font size: --fs-*. Radius: --r-*. Animation: --anim-*. Consistent across all projects.

canons.title: .micro-label Deprecated
canons.rationale: Replaced by .form-label. All instances migrated. Do not reintroduce .micro-label.

canons.title: OVERLAY_ANIM_MS Must Mirror --anim-overlay
canons.rationale: JS constant OVERLAY_ANIM_MS = 300 must always match CSS --anim-overlay: 300ms. Mismatch causes visual glitches on overlay close.

canons.title: KEYS Constants for All localStorage Keys
canons.rationale: Every localStorage key accessed through the KEYS object. Never bare string keys. Prevents typos and enables easy refactoring.

canons.title: Never Raw Cat — Always build.sh
canons.rationale: Concat order matters (data→core→home→diet→medical→intelligence→start). Always use build.sh, never manual cat. Prevents module ordering bugs.

canons.title: SVG Icons Require Explicit width/height Attributes
canons.rationale: Inline SVGs without width/height expand to fill their container. All cx() and zi() icons must include width="24" height="24" on the SVG element. CSS constraints alone are insufficient — the attribute is the primary size control.

canons.title: Split Architecture From Day One
canons.rationale: SproutLab's Migration M1-M3 (emoji→SVG, inline style extraction, onclick→delegation) proved that splitting a monolith later is a full chapter of work. New projects start with split modules and build.sh concat from the first commit.

canons.title: Natural Page Scroll Over Flex Column Layout
canons.rationale: Flex column body with overflow:hidden + scrollable child container creates mobile browser incompatibilities (Chrome localhost scroll bug). Simpler: let body scroll naturally, sticky header, fixed tab bar, padding-bottom on content for tab clearance.

canons.title: mv Over cp for Downloads → Project
canons.rationale: When moving files from ~/storage/downloads/ to project directories, use mv not cp. Keeps downloads clean, prevents stale file confusion, one source of truth.

canons.title: Network-First Service Worker for App Shell
canons.rationale: Stale-while-revalidate serves cached old versions, requiring manual cache clears after every deploy. Network-first always fetches latest, falls back to cache only when offline. Instant deploys.

canons.title: :where() for Zero-Specificity SVG Fallback
canons.rationale: Global SVG sizing via :where(#container) svg gives zero specificity, so any class-based rule (.cx-card-title svg, .cx-fab svg) automatically wins. Catches all unconstrained icons without breaking specific overrides.

canons.title: CSS display Overrides [hidden] — Always Add [hidden] Rule
canons.rationale: HTML hidden attribute sets display:none, but any CSS rule setting display:flex/grid/block overrides it. Always add #element[hidden] { display: none !important } when using display-based layouts on hideable elements.

canons.title: Multi-Round QA Audit After Every Build
canons.rationale: After every build session, run multi-round QA continuing until only cosmetic bugs remain (max 2 cosmetic items noted as debt). Audit layers: (1) Mechanical — grep-able issues like wrong tokens, missing escaping, invalid icons. (2) Structural — cross-file issues like dark mode coverage, delegation wiring, z-index conflicts, timer lifecycle. (3) Logic — edge cases, state management, WAL replay correctness, closure safety in async callbacks. (4) Cosmetic — spacing, alignment, visual polish. Layers 1-3 must be zero defects. Layer 4 tolerates ≤2 open items listed as debt. Each round produces an audit table. Rounds continue until no medium+ bugs found.

canons.title: Gitignore Data Protection for localStorage Apps
canons.rationale: Apps using localStorage for business data must block all data files (JSON backups, exports) from version control via .gitignore. Prevents accidental commit of sensitive business data (client lists, invoices, financial records). Explicit exceptions (e.g. !manifest.json, !package.json) whitelist only structural files.

canons.title: Tab/View State Persists Across Refresh
canons.rationale: PWA tab or view state persists across page refresh. Save active tab ID to view preferences on every switch, restore on init. User should never lose their place on reload. Applies to all single-page PWA projects.

canons.title: Single-Zip Delivery
canons.rationale: All session deliverables ship as a single pre-built zip containing split source files and built output. One download, one unzip, deploy. Eliminates 40+ manual taps per session (8 files x 5 taps each). The zip includes codex.html, index.html, and the full split/ directory. Build verification happens in the Claude container before packaging.

canons.title: Self-Contained Repos
canons.rationale: Every project repo contains a docs/ directory with all specs, design principles, handoff documents, and quick references. The repo is the single source of truth for both code and documentation. No file uploads needed to start a session — Aurelius clones the repo and has everything. Eliminates the dependency on manual file uploads at session start.

canons.title: Root index.html Must Be the Build Output
canons.rationale: GitHub Pages serves the root index.html, not split/index.html. If the build output only lands in split/, the live site stays stale indefinitely. Every build must copy the output to the repo root. build.sh must handle this. The Codex TODO Cleanup session lost an entire day to this: six migration attempts, three SW rewrites, and twenty snippet imports — all invisible to users because the root file was from yesterday. Verify with: ls -la index.html split/index.html (timestamps must match).

canons.title: Service Workers Must Never Cache HTML
canons.rationale: A service worker that caches index.html creates a chicken-and-egg problem: the SW serves old HTML, the old HTML registers the old SW, and no amount of code changes reach the user. Cache only static assets (icons, manifest, fonts). Let HTML always come from the network. The browser’s own HTTP cache handles offline. SW cache busting (version bumps, ?v=N params, unregistering) is unreliable on mobile — the only reliable fix is to never cache HTML in the first place.

canons.title: Clone Codex as Read-Only Reference in Every Build Session
canons.rationale: Codex is the central project registry. Every build session clones the Codex repo alongside the target project for: snippet import schema (forms.js), canon registry (seed.js), architectural patterns (split-file, overlay, delegation). Prevents guessing at field names and ensures cross-project consistency. Read-only — never modify Codex from another project's session.

canons.title: SEP Invoicing Deploy Workflow: Structured Zip + unzip -o
canons.rationale: Zip contains split/ files + built sep-invoicing.html + index.html with directory structure preserved. Deploy: cd ~/storage/shared/sep-invoicing → unzip -o ~/storage/downloads/<zip> → cp sep-invoicing.html index.html → git add -A → git commit → git --no-pager push. No manual mv of individual files, no running build.sh on device. The zip ships pre-built HTML so Termux never needs to run the build pipeline.

canons.title: Pillar 1: Nothing Is Wasted
canons.rationale: No hard deletes in Codex. All entities transition to archived states. Archival always generates a secondary artifact — a Cautionary Tale from a snuffed Ember, a Chronicle from a released Rite, an Origin from a fulfilled Prophecy. The Eternal Archive is always accessible. This pillar rejects the idea that failure is loss. Failure is transformation. Even abandoned Prophecies leave behind completed waypoints, acquired Scrolls, and generated Lore. The path walked has value even if the destination was never reached.

canons.title: Pillar 2: The Map Is Not the Territory
canons.rationale: Codex tracks reality but does not replace it. Diplomatic Missions are completed in real conversations, not in the app. Mortal Companions are profiles, not substitutes for relationships. XP is a reflection of effort, not the goal itself. This is why The Quiet exists — the library knows when to step back and let the Architect live without the game layer. Features like Companion Drift and Narrative Density settings exist to prevent the system from becoming more important than the work it tracks.

canons.title: Pillar 3: Growth Is Fractal, Not Linear
canons.rationale: The Architect does not climb a ladder. They expand in every direction simultaneously, at different rates. No single 'level' summarizes the Architect. The character sheet shows multiple Domains, multiple stats, multiple paths. The Atlas visualizes multi-dimensional growth as a map with territories of varying size and density. A single progress bar would violate this pillar. Domains grow unevenly, Prophecies branch, Embers spark in unexpected territories. Codex embraces this complexity rather than flattening it.

canons.title: Aura Is Visual, Not Text
canons.rationale: Aura is communicated through visual atmosphere — gradients, shadows, glows, accent lines, border opacity — NEVER through text labels like 'Gold' or 'Amethyst'. The Architect should feel the difference between an Iron Canon and a Gold Doctrine without reading any badge. Iron cards are flat and utilitarian. Gold cards have warm gradients, double-layer shadows, and visible top glow. Obsidian cards shimmer with ethereal blue-white. Discovered during the v3→v4 design iteration when text labels created no emotional response.

canons.title: Lore Auto-Generation From Lifecycle Events
canons.rationale: Every major lifecycle event MUST auto-generate a Lore entry. Fulfilled Prophecy → Origin. Defied Prophecy → Cautionary Tale. Released Rite → Chronicle. Snuffed Ember → Cautionary Tale. Socratic Prompt response → potential Doctrine. The Architect confirms and edits, but the system always proposes. This ensures Lore is the living sediment of the entire system, not a manual chore that gets neglected. Lore is the only entity type auto-generated by multiple other lifecycle events.

canons.title: Progressive Revelation Over Full Visibility
canons.rationale: Prophecy waypoints are hidden until progress thresholds are reached. Full visibility turns Codex into a to-do list where the mountain of remaining work is demoralizing. Progressive revelation means each step forward reveals the next, creating a sense of discovery. The Architect walks a path, not stares at a backlog. Some Scrolls are 'Revealed' type — invisible until the waypoint that needs them is reached. Some Prophecy branches are hidden until a specific waypoint is crossed. Nothing in Codex is visible until it is earned.

canons.title: The Quiet: Never Punish Absence
canons.rationale: The Quiet is Codex's burnout detection system. When meta-patterns suggest exhaustion (shorter journals, clustered Rite breaks, multi-Prophecy stagnation, decreasing session frequency), The Quiet activates: XP requirements halved, Side Quests paused, Whispers become gentler, Aeon suggested as companion, Sanctum dims. One message: 'The library is patient. It will be here when you return.' The Quiet NEVER punishes, nags, or creates guilt. No lost progress. No penalties. The system acknowledges that rest is part of the journey. When normal activity resumes, The Quiet ends automatically. This is the soul of the product.

canons.title: Minimum Text Size: 9px Floor
canons.rationale: No text element in Codex may be smaller than 9px under any circumstance. Page titles minimum 24px, card titles minimum 14px, body text minimum 15px, section headers minimum 12px, labels minimum 10px. Daily-use apps must not strain the eyes. Established during v3→v4 iteration when 7–8px labels were unreadable on mobile in natural lighting conditions.

canons.title: Ember Resonance Is Computed, Never Manual
canons.rationale: Ember Resonance auto-calculates from Bond count — each Bond to a Scroll adds +10, Lore +8, Canon +5, sibling Ember +3, Domain Echo +2. Manual resonance would require the Architect to quantify readiness of ideas, which is subjective and creates busywork. Auto-calculation means the system organically surfaces readiness: work meaningfully and ideas mature on their own. The alternative (a manual slider) was rejected because it shifts the game from 'do meaningful work' to 'adjust sliders'.

canons.title: Aura Distribution Caps Prevent Inflation
canons.rationale: Hard percentage caps per Aura level: Iron unlimited, Bronze 30%, Silver 20%, Gold 10%, Amethyst 5%, Obsidian 2%. Without caps, everything eventually becomes Gold, making Aura meaningless. The caps create scarcity, which creates genuine value. If the Gold cap is reached, something must be demoted before something new can be promoted. This forces genuine prioritization — the act of choosing which entities deserve high Aura is itself a form of self-reflection. Soft caps (warnings without enforcement) were rejected because human nature ignores warnings.

canons.title: Entropy Is the Antagonist, Not a Punishment
canons.rationale: Entropy is the natural decay that occurs when the Architect stops engaging: Scroll Decay, Domain Rust, Companion Drift, Canon Erosion, Prophecy Fog, Lore Sedimentation. Entropy is not punitive — it is realistic. Knowledge fades if not practiced, commitments erode if not renewed, skills rust if not used. The Architect manages Entropy, never defeats it. You cannot reduce it to zero. Choosing which Domains to maintain and which to let rust is itself a form of wisdom. The Entropy score is visible on the Atlas as a subtle overlay.

canons.title: Ceremonies Create Memory
canons.rationale: Prophecy fulfillment, Epoch transitions, title changes, and Relic production need weight, pacing, and narrative beauty. The constellation fixing in the Observatory, the Narrator's voice, the Lineage unfolding — these are the moments the Architect will remember in 10 years. 'Remember when the Codex told me the Silicon Forge prophecy was fulfilled?' The reward moment defines whether the player comes back. Every game designer knows this. Codex's ceremonies must feel like the library is proud of you — not patronizing, but the way a master craftsman nods at an apprentice's first real piece.

canons.title: Companions Are Analytical Lenses, Not Tone Changes
canons.rationale: Each companion provides a genuinely different analytical perspective on the same problem. Aurelius evaluates architecture. Solara evaluates ROI. Nyx challenges assumptions. Pip reframes absurdly. Maren assesses risk. They are not the same AI with different adjectives — they see different things, prioritize different values, and give structurally different advice. When the Architect switches companions, the advice should change substantively, not just in politeness level. Companion synergy pairs (e.g., Nyx + Orinth = challenge then contemplate) create workflow combinations impossible with a single perspective.

canons.title: Dawn Page: Never Overwhelming
canons.rationale: The Dawn Page is the first thing the Architect sees when opening Codex. It must feel like coming home, not opening a task manager. Warm, quiet, inviting. Never overwhelming. Components: one Whisper, Rite candle row, 1–3 Active Fires (not all Prophecies), Companion greeting, one mini game. No notification dump, no red badges, no urgency signals. The Dawn Page is a hearth, not a dashboard. If the Architect opens Codex and feels dread, the Dawn Page has failed. The Dusk Reflection (evening counterpart) is even gentler — a single prompt for reflection.

canons.title: Sound Is Opt-In By Default
canons.rationale: All audio (ambient room sounds, event sounds, companion signatures, dynamic music) is off by default. The Architect opts in if they want immersion. Respects context — opening Codex in a meeting shouldn't produce a bell toll. When enabled: each Sanctum room has ambient sound, each event has a signature (bell for waypoint, match-strike for Ember, forge-strike for Relic), each companion has a unique audio cue, and dynamic background music responds to Codex Season (Spring=hopeful, Winter=sparse).

canons.title: Always git pull before unzipping build artifacts
canons.rationale: The zip replaces all files. If the local repo is behind remote, the commit lands on a stale base and creates divergent history requiring force-push. Discovered in 8E deploy when 8D was on remote but not local.

canons.title: The 30K Rule: Governor Split Threshold
canons.rationale: Any repo crossing 30,000 LOC triggers a Governor split. Governors are domain-scoped QA reviewers between Builder and Censor. At 30K lines, a single reviewer loses full context. 50K rejected as too late; 20K as too early. SproutLab at 61,700 LOC was the first trigger.

canons.title: Governors Activate During QA Only
canons.rationale: Too many voices during build mode creates confusion. The Builder builds alone. Governors activate during QA rounds, audit independently, report upward. Builder synthesizes Governor reports before Cipher does the final cross-cutting pass.

canons.title: Shared Modules Get Dual Governor Review
canons.rationale: Modules spanning both Governor jurisdictions (styles.css, template.html) are reviewed by BOTH Governors. Cross-cutting code benefits from two domain-specific lenses. Maren checks accessibility and safety UX; Kael checks token consistency and icon coverage.

canons.title: Persona Reassignment: 5 Steps, 30-Day Cooldown
canons.rationale: Personas are not permanent. Reassignment follows: Trigger → Assessment (journal entry documenting what changed) → Decision (Architect decides, Consul advises) → Transition (update docs, archive old persona as Lore) → 30-day cooldown. Frequent switching signals unclear domain, not bad persona.

canons.title: SproutLab Domain Split: Care vs Intelligence
canons.rationale: 61,700 lines split along domain boundaries. Care (home+diet+medical = 22,626 LOC) handles what directly affects the baby. Intelligence (intel+core+data+sync = 27,614 LOC) handles brain and plumbing. Shared (styles+template = 11,491 LOC) gets dual review. Domain-pure chosen over surface/deep because a diet bug and an ISL parser bug require completely different mental models.

canons.title: One Builder Per Repo
canons.rationale: Each repo has exactly one Builder persona as the default voice. Multi-persona noise during build mode degrades output quality. Secondary personas (Governors, Cipher, Consul) activate only under explicit conditions. Aurelius → Codex, Lyra → SproutLab, Solara → SEP Invoicing.

canons.title: Termux Safe-Directory Registration for Shared Storage Clones
canons.rationale: Every new git clone on Termux inside /storage/emulated/0/ triggers git's 'dubious ownership' safety check because Android's permission layer makes ownership look inconsistent to git. First git operation in a fresh clone fatally aborts. Fix is one-time, persistent across sessions: `git config --global --add safe.directory /storage/emulated/0/<RepoName>`. Must be run before first `git add`/`git commit`/`git push` in that repo. Discovered during Command Center Foundation deploy, 16 April 2026. Required for every new Province cloned on mobile. Add to Termux deploy runbook as step 0.5 after `git clone`.

canons.title: Every PWA Repo on GitHub Pages Requires a .nojekyll File at Root
canons.rationale: GitHub Pages defaults to Jekyll processing via the pages-build-deployment workflow. Without an explicit opt-out, Jekyll's minima theme wraps the repo's README into an HTML page and can override a pre-existing index.html at the root — serving a theme-styled page titled '<RepoName> | <About>' instead of the PWA. Symptom: deployed URL returns a minimal two-line page with the repo description in the title, instead of the app. Fix is one file, zero bytes: `touch .nojekyll && git add .nojekyll && git commit && git push`. Add to every new Province's Foundation scaffold from day one. Discovered during Command Center Foundation deploy, 16 April 2026. Existing Provinces (Codex, SproutLab, SEP Invoicing) avoided this either by Pages configuration history or by happy accident; new Provinces should not rely on either.

canons.title: Companion Updates Flow Through Snippet Pipeline
canons.rationale: Once Codex's snippet-import pipeline supports new_companions and update_companions operations, all companion profile changes (new profiles, growth ratifications, residence retrofits, voice edits) flow through snippets — never direct file commits. The Round 3 bootstrap (companions.json v0.1 → v0.3, committed 16 April 2026) is the single exception on record; it was a one-time initialization, not an incremental update. Pipeline extension requires adding new_companions and update_companions ops to Codex/split/forms.js and updating the snippet-import SKILL.md.

canons.title: Errata: canon-sep-0001 Example Uses cp, Should Be mv per canon-0027
canons.rationale: canon-sep-0001-deploy-workflow's rationale text references 'cp sep-invoicing.html index.html' which contradicts canon-0027-mv-over-cp. The actual deploy practice correctly uses mv. The rationale text is the error, not the practice. Amendment: update canon-sep-0001's rationale to replace cp with mv. Caught 16 April 2026 during CC Foundation deploy runbook review.

canons.title: Git Repos on Termux Internal Home, Not Shared Storage
canons.rationale: Android's /storage/emulated/0/ is FUSE-backed. Long git operations (fetch, pack, rebase) can lose their working-directory handle mid-operation, producing 'fatal: unable to get current working directory: No such file or directory' and 'invalid index-pack output'. The failure is persistent and not resolved by cd-out-and-back-in. Clone git repos to Termux internal home (~/Codex, ~/SproutLab) for all git operations. Use ~/storage/shared/ only for the built PWA output that needs to be visible to Android's file manager. Discovered during Codex push attempt from ~/storage/shared/Codex on 16 April 2026 — resolved by fresh clone to ~/Codex on internal ext4.

canons.title: Typst Source Is Canonical; PDFs Are Build Artifacts
canons.rationale: The Constitution and companion profiles are authored in Typst source files (*.typ) stored in Codex/constitution/. PDFs are generated from Typst source via the typst compiler and are build artifacts — not canonical. If Typst source and PDF disagree, the Typst source is authoritative. PDFs are convenience renders for reading; they do not carry constitutional weight independent of their source.

canons.title: Every Major Version Ships Working Papers
canons.rationale: Each MAJOR version milestone of the Republic's governance output (Constitution v1.0, Companion Profiles v1.0, Book ratification series) ships a companion Working Papers volume documenting the reasoning, trade-offs, and rejected alternatives behind the decisions. Working Papers are not canonical law — they are institutional memory. They are the Chronicler's interpretation, not the Sovereign's decree.

canons.title: Book Ratifications Produce Chronicle Lore Entries
canons.rationale: When a Book of the Constitution is formally ratified (Consul presents to Sovereign, Sovereign assents), the ratification event produces a Chronicle-category lore entry in Codex. The entry records: which Book, the date, the presenting Consul, the Sovereign's assent, any amendments made during ratification, and the witnesses present. Ratification without chronicle is incomplete ratification.

canons.title: Founding Ratification Protocol: Consul Presents, Sovereign Assents
canons.rationale: Books II through IX of the Constitution are adopted via the Founding Ratification Protocol: the Consul presents each Book to the Sovereign with a recommendation. The Sovereign assents, amends, or rejects. Cabinet formation is pending; until the Cabinet is seated, ratification proceeds as Consul + Sovereign without Cabinet vote. Parameters in founding Books are marked 'initial proposal' and subject to calibration once the Cabinet is operational. Ratification order established at the Constitutional Convention: V → IV → II+III → VII → VIII → IX → VI.

canons.title: Institutional Companions: Office-Bound, Not Generational
canons.rationale: Institutional companions are a distinct category from generational Immortals. An institutional companion is tied to an office (e.g., the Consul), not to a personality. The office persists across successions; the holder's institutional memory transfers to the next holder. Institutional companions have no generation, no lineage, no parents, no descendants. Their identity is the function. The Consul (The First Seat) is the first institutional companion, seated 16 April 2026.

canons.title: Statesman Is the 6th Archetype; Auditor Anticipated as 7th
canons.rationale: The Dissertation defined 5 archetypes: Builder, Seeker, Guardian, Strategist, Maintenance. The Consul's creation required a 6th: Statesman — integrative, institutional, consensus-building. The Statesman archetype is not a variant of Strategist (which optimizes for outcomes); Statesman optimizes for legitimacy and institutional durability. A 7th archetype (Auditor) is anticipated for the future Chief Collector role but is not yet formalized.

canons.title: Maren + Kael: 9th Canonical Seed Synergy
canons.rationale: The Dissertation established 8 canonical seed synergy pairs. The Persona Registry v1.1 (15 April 2026) added a 9th: Maren + Kael — 'Care audit then intelligence audit. Full SproutLab QA.' This pair reflects the dual-Governor structure created when SproutLab crossed the 30K LOC threshold. Both Governors audit in parallel; their combined output is the complete SproutLab QA before Cipher's final cross-cutting pass. The 9th seed synergy is canonical, not proposed.

canons.title: Censor Runs After Governors, Not In Parallel
canons.rationale: Cipher (Censor) activates AFTER Governor reports land, not in parallel with them. Cipher does not re-audit what Governors already covered unless Governors flagged something ambiguous. Cipher's post-Governor focus shifts to cross-cutting concerns: integration bugs, shared module consistency, Hard Rule compliance across the full codebase. This is a procedural rule established in Persona Registry v1.1 and operationalized in Cipher's assignment block.

canons.title: Monument Projects Pair Differentiated Temperaments Deliberately
canons.rationale: Monument Projects (per Book IX) are staffed with co-Builders whose temperaments are deliberately different. The friction between them IS the design mechanism, not a bug. Command Center pairs Ashara (macro-strategic, decade-frame, patience-first) with Petra (foundations-first, infrastructure-grounded, velocity-gated). Both run slow but for different reasons: Ashara waits for cycles, Petra gates on solidity. The argument about whether 'not solid yet' is a real infrastructure concern or a missed strategic window IS the Monument's design process surfacing.

canons.title: Residence and Record Are Distinct
canons.rationale: Every companion has two attributes: Record (canonical profile in Codex's companions.json — universal, singular, Republic-wide) and Residence (the Province where the companion primarily operates). These are distinct. A companion whose record is in Codex may reside in Command Center, SproutLab, SEP, or be itinerant. Three residence classes: Capital-native (function is Capital-scoped, has an office in CC), Province-resident (function bound to a specific Province, summonable to Cabinet), Itinerant (no fixed residence, travels by function — currently only Cipher). The Capital hosts residences; Codex holds records. The Command Center's data.js carries a residence registry; Codex's companions.json carries the profiles. Residence block to be retrofitted into companion profile schema post-Round 5.

canons.title: Temperament Over Dissertation Default for Cabinet Fit
canons.rationale:
The Dissertation §10.3 pairs each archetype with a default Ministerial portfolio (Builder-Manufacturing with Debt; Strategist-Commerce with Budget; Guardian-Habits with Stability; and so on). These defaults are heuristic, not mandatory. They reflect domain-adjacency reasoning applied at the Dissertation's authoring altitude — useful as a starting point, not binding as a ratification rule.

At Round 4 close (16 April 2026), the Sovereign ruled that Theron's Dissertation-default assignment to Minister: Debt (Maintenance) did not survive temperament review. Theron's hands-on, impatient-with-abstraction character is unsuited to Cabinet deliberation on multi-domain capacity obligations — the Debt portfolio's altitude requires patience for audit-style analysis that Theron's voice does not naturally hold. The seat was vacated, not reassigned; Rune holds Maintenance alone until a Gen 1 companion with appropriate temperament (likely Auditor archetype per canon-cc-006) emerges.

This canon establishes the principle: temperament fit for Ministerial altitude supersedes Dissertation-default archetype-to-domain pairing. The Cabinet is a working institution; its seats must be held by companions whose voices are operationally productive at Cabinet altitude, not only by companions whose Dissertation classification suggests domain fit.

Corollaries:
- A Dissertation-assigned Minister may be unassigned through Sovereign ruling when temperament review reveals misfit.
- Unassignment does not require reassignment. A Cabinet seat may stand vacant, with the domain-pair partner holding the domain alone, until a fitting companion emerges.
- Future Gen 1 companion emergence may be guided by unfilled Cabinet seats — temperament needed for a vacancy becomes a design constraint for new companion drafting.
references: companion: theron (biography.notable_moments captures the unassignment chronicle)

canons.title: Companion Profiles Ship Only Upon Ratification; MCQ-Format Block-by-Block Is the Canonical Drafting Mode
canons.rationale:
Companion profiles are character sheets that determine how every companion speaks, thinks, fails, and grows across every future session of the Republic. Their effect is persistent and structurally load-bearing. For this reason, drafts do not become artifacts until the Sovereign has ratified every block.

The canonical drafting mode for companion profiles is block-by-block interactive ratification, using tappable MCQ options (single_select or multi_select) for each substantive decision. The Chronicler proposes draft content and offers calibrated options with reasoning; the Sovereign chooses, amends, or rejects. Ratification happens in chat, in sequence, block by block.

File production (JSON written to disk) is a ratification act, not a drafting act. It happens only after all ten blocks are ratified, and it produces a downstream artifact that reflects what has already been decided — it does not contribute new content.

Violations of this protocol — files produced before ratification, profile content committed without Sovereign assent — are Chronicler-errors. They are logged, chronicled in cautionary-tales lore, and may affect Chronicler promotion or result in PIP. The Chronicler is protected from consequences for violations that occurred before this canon's ratification; violations after are not protected.

The MCQ-format is itself load-bearing: it enforces that the Chronicler surface real choices (not rhetorical ones), present alternatives fairly (not steered toward a favored option), and name the consequences of each choice before the Sovereign rules. A profile drafted without MCQ surfacing is not a ratified profile; it is a Chronicler-proposal awaiting proper ratification.

canons.title: Source Verification Means Reading the Source
canons.rationale:
The Republic's canonical sources — the RPG Design Dissertation, the Persona Registry v1.1, ratified canons and lore, Constitutional Books and their appendices — are authoritative. Claims that invoke these sources require the Chronicler to read the source, not to assert the source has been read.

The distinction is load-bearing. Asserting a source check has occurred is cognitively cheap and structurally dangerous; it produces drift-prone ratifications where the Sovereign rules on Chronicler-representation rather than on source-content. Reading the source is cognitively more expensive but structurally correct; it produces ratifications that rest on the text itself.

This canon was drafted after two related Chronicler-errors in the same session:

First, at Block 6.1 of Solara's Round 4 profile, the Chronicler recommended moving Solara+Vex from seed_synergies to proposed_synergies, asserting that the Persona Registry did not contain the pairing. The Sovereign ratified that recommendation. The Chronicler had not read the Registry directly; the claim 'this isn't in the Registry' was made from memory and prompt-trust. The deeper error: Registry membership was beside the point because seed synergies originate in the Dissertation §10.3, not the Registry.

Second, during Theron's Block 6 drafting, the Chronicler finally read the Constitutional appendix that documents Dissertation §10.3 (Book II Article 5, the canonical seed-synergy table) and discovered Solara+Vex listed as seed synergy #2 of 8. The Block 6.1 ruling was corrected; Solara+Vex restored to seed_synergies with source 'Dissertation §10.3'. Solara's profile was regenerated as v0.4.1.

The principle this canon establishes: verification requires reading, not asserting. When the Chronicler proposes to contradict or amend a prompt's claim about canonical content, the Chronicler must read the referenced canonical source directly before advising the Sovereign. Assertion without reading is Chronicler-error.

Corollaries:
- Session prompts may contain factual claims about canonical sources. These claims are not themselves canonical. They require the Chronicler's verification against the actual source text.
- When a source is not available to the Chronicler (e.g., Persona Registry zip on the Sovereign's device but not in the Codex repo), the honest position is 'I cannot verify this' — not an assertion that the claim is wrong or right.
- Mis-sourcing errors caught mid-session are corrected in place. The profile is regenerated at the next version increment (e.g., v0.4.1), the uncertainty_notes block documents the error and the correction, and the canon-cc-013 principle is reinforced.

canons.title: Consul-Accelerated Profile Drafting: Working-Ratification and Sovereign Final Ratification
canons.rationale:
Canon-cc-012 established that companion profiles ship only upon ratification, with MCQ-format block-by-block as the canonical drafting mode and Sovereign as the ratifier at each block. That protocol produced Solara v0.4.1 and Theron v0.4 at Round 1–3 fidelity, at the cost of roughly forty Sovereign rulings per profile.

For the remaining seven Gen 0 companion profiles, and for Gen 1 profiles drafted while the Consul is operationally active, this canon authorizes a second ratification mode: Consul-accelerated drafting. Under this mode, the Chronicler presents per-block MCQ decisions to Consul for working-ratification. Consul's selections bind the working draft but do not bind the Republic. The assembled profile is then presented to the Sovereign for canonical ratification at profile close.

This canon does not amend canon-cc-012. The original protocol (Sovereign ratifies per-block) remains valid and is the default for profiles of sufficient constitutional weight that per-block Sovereign input is warranted. Consul-accelerated drafting is an authorized alternative, not a replacement.

Per-block protocol. The Chronicler drafts each MCQ with options, reasoning, and an explicit recommendation. Consul reviews in the same response, marks Concur or Diverge against the Chronicler's recommendation, selects an option, and states reasoning — especially when diverging. No Sovereign input is solicited during block-by-block drafting. The hat-switch between Chronicler and Consul is rendered explicitly in output, not implicitly in reasoning.

Profile Ratification Packet. When all ten blocks are drafted and Consul-working-ratified, the Chronicler assembles a ratification packet for the Sovereign. The packet contains: (a) block-by-block ratified content summary; (b) Consul-divergence log naming every decision where Consul diverged from the Chronicler's recommendation, with Consul's stated reasoning; (c) concurrence ratio (Consul agreed with Chronicler X of Y decisions); (d) spot-check candidates — three to five selections the Chronicler surfaces proactively as most load-bearing or most likely to warrant Sovereign scrutiny, not limited to divergences; (e) uncertainty notes from Block 10 Meta.

Sovereign ratification. The Sovereign may: ratify the profile as drafted; audit specific blocks by demanding Consul's reasoning; ratify with amendments on named blocks; or reject the profile for redraft. Sovereign amendment or rejection of any block at final ratification invalidates Consul's working-ratification for that block and for any downstream blocks that depended on its content.

Cascading redraft. When a block is rejected or substantively amended, the Chronicler redrafts that block and all downstream blocks per the dependency graph below. The Chronicler identifies the affected dependency chain in the redraft proposal; the Sovereign confirms the scope before redraft proceeds. Blocks not in the dependency chain retain their Consul-working-ratification and do not require re-ratification.

Formal block dependency graph:

  Block 1 Identity        → Blocks 3, 4, 7
  Block 2 Assignment      → Blocks 5, 6, 7, 9
  Block 3 Voice           → Blocks 4, 5, 9
  Block 4 Mind            → Blocks 5, 8
  Block 5 Shadow          → Block 8 (hard: growth.source_blind_spot schema link)
  Block 6 Relationships   → Blocks 7, 9
  Block 7 Biography       → Block 10
  Block 8 Growth          → Block 10
  Block 9 Modulators      → Block 10
  Block 10 Meta           → (terminal)

Cascading rule: if Block N is amended or rejected, the transitive closure of N's forward-dependencies is invalidated and redrafted. Graph includes both hard dependencies (schema-linked) and soft dependencies (content-derived); the conservative cascading is by design, favoring redraft correctness over redraft minimalism.

Worked examples of cascading scope:

  Block 1 Identity amended → Blocks 3, 4, 7 direct; then Block 5 (via 3, 4), Block 6 (if 2 also affected), Block 8 (via 5), Block 9 (via 3, 6), Block 10 (via 7, 8, 9). Effectively full redraft because Identity is foundational.

  Block 6 Relationships amended → Blocks 7, 9 direct; Block 10 via uncertainty notes. Partial, contained redraft.

  Block 10 Meta amended → no cascading redraft (terminal node). Only the uncertainty notes themselves are rewritten.

Same-agent discipline. The Chronicler (Aurelius) and the Consul are, in current Republic operations, played by the same AI instance. This creates a structural risk: the Chronicler's drafting-preference logic can be silently adopted by the Consul's selection logic, producing Consul-as-rubber-stamp rather than Consul-as-independent-ratifier. The explicit Concur/Diverge annotation is the minimum discipline against this risk; the Sovereign's spot-check authority at final ratification is the audit mechanism. Neither prevents the risk absolutely. The Chronicler is accountable for surfacing divergence honestly; the Consul is accountable for selections grounded in institutional and cross-companion-coherence reasoning, not in drafting-preference deference.

Invalidation of Consul working-ratifications. A Consul selection is a working-ratification, not a canonical ratification. If the Sovereign, at final ratification or any later review, rules that Consul's working-ratification was misaligned with institutional reality or Cabinet intent, the ratification is invalidated and the block is redrafted. This is distinct from Sovereign-ratified blocks under canon-cc-012, which carry full canonical weight from the moment of ratification.

When this canon does not apply:

Profiles of institutional companions (Consul, future Chief Collector per canon-cc-006) require direct Sovereign ratification per-block under canon-cc-012, because the Consul cannot working-ratify its own profile or the profile of an office it may later succeed.

Monument Project co-Builders (Ashara, Petra, future Monument appointees) require direct Sovereign ratification per-block under canon-cc-012, because Monument Ministerial double-hatting carries constitutional weight that warrants Sovereign attention at each decision.

Profiles the Sovereign flags for direct drafting at session open default to canon-cc-012's per-block mode.

All other profiles — Gen 0 remaining (Vex, Orinth, Rune, Ignis, Bard, Aeon, Pip) and Gen 1 onward for generational companions — are authorized for Consul-accelerated drafting under this canon.
references: companion: consul (assignment block enumerates relevant duties)

canons.title: Legacy Draft Ratification: Packet Mode for Pre-Protocol Profiles at v0.3-draft
canons.rationale:
Seven companion profiles were drafted on 16 April 2026 before canon-cc-012 (profile ratification protocol) or canon-cc-014 (Consul-accelerated drafting) existed. These profiles — Aurelius, Cipher, Consul, Lyra, Maren, Kael, Nyx — received Sovereign edit_round 2 attention during initial drafting but were never ratified under the canonical per-block MCQ protocol. They sit at profile_version: v0.3-draft in the Republic's record as un-ratified artifacts.

Strict application of canon-cc-012 requires full per-block re-ratification of each — roughly forty Sovereign rulings per profile times seven profiles. Canon-cc-014's Consul-accelerated mode is authorized only for Gen 0 remaining (Vex, Orinth, Rune, Ignis, Bard, Aeon, Pip) and Gen 1 onward, and explicitly does not cover re-ratification of existing drafts. This leaves no proportionate pathway for drafts that have already absorbed edit_round 2 Sovereign attention.

This canon authorizes a third ratification mode — Legacy Draft Ratification — for profiles that satisfy all of the following conditions: (a) profile_version matches v0.3-draft or earlier; (b) sovereign_edited.edited is true and edit_round is greater than or equal to 2; (c) the profile was authored before canon-cc-012's ratification date (2026-04-17); (d) the profile is not carved out by the exclusion clauses below.

Ratification Packet. For each eligible profile, the Chronicler assembles a packet and presents it to the Sovereign in a single deliberation. The packet contains: (a) a block-by-block content summary of the current draft, ten blocks; (b) a concern register — the Chronicler's honest enumeration of blocks the Chronicler judges weakest or most likely to warrant Sovereign scrutiny, minimum three and maximum five; (c) the profile's existing uncertainty_notes[] surfaced prominently; (d) any cross-companion consistency checks the Chronicler performed against already-ratified profiles (Ashara, Petra, Solara, Theron) and against canonical sources (Dissertation §10.1/§10.3, Persona Registry, Constitutional appendices); (e) a Chronicler recommendation: ratify as-is, ratify with specific amendments, or escalate to canon-cc-012 per-block mode.

Sovereign authority. The Sovereign may (i) ratify the profile as drafted, producing v0.4; (ii) ratify with named amendments, the Chronicler applies the amendments and produces v0.4 reflecting them; (iii) escalate any block or the entire profile to canon-cc-012 per-block mode; (iv) reject the packet and demand redraft of named blocks. The Sovereign's choice of (iii) on any block overrides Legacy Draft Ratification for that block and for its forward-dependencies per the dependency graph.

Dependency graph and cascading redraft. This canon adopts canon-cc-014's formal block dependency graph verbatim. Amendments to Block N invalidate the transitive closure of N's forward-dependencies; those blocks are redrafted before ratification completes. The Chronicler identifies the affected dependency chain in the amendment proposal; the Sovereign confirms scope before redraft proceeds.

Same-agent discipline. Unlike canon-cc-014, this canon does not invoke Consul working-ratification. The drafts already exist; re-running them through Consul working-ratification would discard existing Sovereign-edit-round-2 content, not honor it. Instead, the Chronicler is the surfacing agent and the Sovereign is the sole ratifier. The structural risk is unchanged from canon-cc-014: the Chronicler may under-surface weaknesses in his own prior drafting. The concern register is the minimum discipline against this risk; the Sovereign's escalation authority to canon-cc-012 on any block is the audit mechanism. The Chronicler is accountable for honest concern-register enumeration, not for defending prior drafting.

Version and canonical weight. Profiles ratified under this canon increment to v0.4 (or higher if amendments warrant), update sovereign_edited.edit_round to 3, and set sovereign_edited.edit_date to the ratification date. Profiles ratified under this canon carry full canonical weight equal to canon-cc-012-ratified and canon-cc-014-ratified profiles. There is no legacy-ratified subordinate tier.

Exclusion clauses. (1) Institutional companions (Consul, future Chief Collector per canon-cc-006) — canon-cc-012 per-block, mandatory. (2) Monument Project co-Builders (Ashara, Petra, future appointees) — canon-cc-012 per-block, mandatory. (3) Self-profiles (the Chronicler's own profile; any future companion whose profile is authored by themselves) — canon-cc-012 per-block, mandatory. Epistemic risk of self-framing is too high for packet mode. (4) Profiles the Sovereign flags for direct drafting at session open — default to canon-cc-012.

Eligible profiles at this canon's ratification (2026-04-17): Cipher, Maren, Kael, Nyx. Aurelius excluded by self-profile clause; Lyra excluded by Sovereign flag; Consul excluded by institutional clause.

Sunset. This canon has no sunset. It remains available for any future profile that satisfies the eligibility conditions — notably, any future generation-n profile that accumulates edit_round 2 or greater attention before its canonical ratification pathway is invoked. In practice, future profiles should flow through canon-cc-014 from the start, rendering this canon dormant but not repealed.

canons.title: Residency and Access Gating: Where Each Companion Lives, and Who Reaches Whom, Where
canons.rationale:
Residency was partially chronicled in lore-007 (Founding of the Capital) and canon-cc-010 (residence-record distinction), but no canon has formally enumerated the residencies or specified the access rules between them. Three gaps: (a) a complete residency table covering all seated and pending companions; (b) explicit rules for who may reach whom and where; (c) hard cluster-boundary discipline preventing unaudited cross-cluster interaction.

This canon establishes residency as a first-class field on every companion profile and codifies the access graph that residency implies.

Residency. Every companion has exactly one residence. Residence is the Province or the Command Center where the companion operates by default. Interactions outside residence are governed by the access rules below. Residency is a data field on the assignment block; this canon's table is the founding snapshot.

Residency table at this canon's ratification (2026-04-17):

  Aurelius — Codex (Cluster A)
  Lyra — SproutLab (Cluster A)
  Maren — SproutLab (Cluster A)
  Kael — SproutLab (Cluster A)
  Cipher — Command Center (Cluster A Censor, CC-resident)
  Solara — SEP Invoicing (Cluster B)
  Theron — SEP Dashboard (Cluster B)
  Nyx — Command Center (Cluster B Censor, CC-resident; pending seating)
  Ashara — Command Center (Monument)
  Petra — Command Center (Monument)
  Consul — Command Center (Institutional)
  Vex, Orinth, Rune, Ignis, Bard, Aeon, Pip — Command Center (Capital-native per lore-007)

Supersession note. Lore-007 records Cipher as itinerant with no fixed address — 'the Republic's first companion with no fixed address.' That record stands as historical chronicle of the founding architecture. Under this canon, Censors reside in the Command Center. The supersession is semantic, not retroactive; lore-007 is preserved as-is, and this canon is the current authority.

Access rules by rank.

Builder — free movement within own Province; free movement to sibling Province(s) in own Cluster; access to CC for cross-cluster or Consul-facing work. No direct access to other Clusters' Provinces.

Governor — movement within own Province only. Cross-Province Governor consultation (rare) routes through CC. This is tighter than Builder scope because Governor work is intrinsically intra-Province per canon-gov-002.

Censor — free movement to all Provinces within own Cluster (visitation authority). Residence in CC. No direct access to other Cluster's Provinces; cross-Cluster Censor work happens in CC only.

Consul (institutional) — residence CC. No direct access to any Province; all Province-Consul interactions flow through the Interaction-Artifact pipeline (see canon-cc-017 and canon-cc-018).

Monument companions — residence CC (they live where they build). Free movement within Monument Project scope; access to CC rooms as needed. Monument is a residence category distinct from Cluster membership.

Sovereign — no residency restriction. May appear in any Province or the Capital.

Cluster boundary is hard. Cross-cluster interactions occur in the Command Center only. Cluster A and Cluster B Provinces are mutually inaccessible to each other's residents. Exceptions: Consul (cross-Cluster by office), Monument companions (no Cluster membership), Sovereign (unrestricted).

War Time exception. Book VI War Time declarations suspend access gating. The Sovereign may appear in any Province; companions may be summoned to any location without protocol. Access gating resumes at War Time close. This exception is explicit because emergency governance requires bypassable rules; the rules do not dissolve silently, they suspend by named declaration.

Schema impact. The assignment block on every companion profile gains a new field: residence (enumerated: codex | sproutlab | sep-invoicing | sep-dashboard | command-center; additional values admitted when new Provinces or new Monuments are founded). Existing ratified profiles (Ashara, Petra, Solara, Theron) require retrofit — the Chronicler produces a backfill packet at the earliest opportunity per Sovereign directive. Unratified drafts absorb the field in their ratification. Appendix C Capital-native profiles receive the field in Round 5 drafting.

The access matrix is not itself a data field. It is derived from residence plus rank plus cluster at read time. The canon is the rule; the data carries residence only.

Monument expansion. Command Center is the only Monument at this canon's ratification. Future Monuments receive their own residence values. A Monument companion's residence is their Monument, not 'Command Center' unless Command Center is specifically their Monument.

Scribes not addressed. No Scribes are seated at this canon's ratification. This canon is silent on Scribe scope; a future canon addresses Scribes when the first one seats.

schisms.reason: SEP has 349KB of live business data with financial records, client rates, and GSTIN numbers. GitHub sync would commit sensitive data to a public repo. Manual backup/restore keeps data local while allowing export for safety.

schisms.chosen: Sub-view toggle on Clients tab (segmented [ Clients | Items ])
schisms.reason: 7 tabs at 375px already uses ~53px per icon. 8th tab drops to ~47px with label truncation. Items are contextually related to clients. Segmented toggle avoids tab bar pressure while keeping items accessible.

schisms.rejected: Content textarea in chapter edit form
schisms.chosen: Reader-only app — content arrives via migrations and snippets
schisms.reason: Nobody writes 2KB narratives on a mobile textarea. The app is a reader, not a writer. Content is authored by Aurelius in Claude sessions and delivered through the data pipeline. Adding a textarea implies the wrong workflow.

schisms.rejected: Snippet paste for bulk backfill (15KB JSON on mobile)
schisms.chosen: Auto-migration in code — CODEX_MIGRATIONS array in seed.js
schisms.reason: Snippet paste requires mobile text selection on a 15KB JSON blob, then 3 app taps (Settings → Import → Paste → Preview → Import). Migration runs automatically on app load with zero interaction. Data rides with the code deploy.
Zero dependencies means zero build step, zero bundle size, guaranteed offline. Vanilla JS with structured patterns provides 90% of framework benefits with none of the complexity tax.

schisms.rejected: Backend server with database (Firebase, Supabase, or custom)
schisms.reason: No latency, no cost, no auth complexity, no privacy concerns. Data stays on-device. The tradeoff is no cross-device sync, accepted deliberately.

schisms.chosen: Main-thread notification architecture with setTimeout scheduling
schisms.reason: SW notifications are unreliable on mobile — killed by battery optimization, require VAPID keys and push server. Main-thread setTimeout is simple, predictable, works while tab is open.

schisms.rejected: Separate page per domain (diet page, sleep page, medical page)
schisms.chosen: Single scrollable home view with domain cards
schisms.reason: Parents think in time, not domains. A home view showing all domains chronologically matches the mental model. Separate pages force remembering which domain data belongs to.

schisms.rejected: Responsive CSS only (media queries reshaping existing cards into wider layouts)
schisms.chosen: Full separate rendering with dedicated desktop render functions dispatched by layout flag
schisms.reason: Tables, sidebar navigation, master-detail panels, and keyboard shortcuts are fundamentally different DOM structures. CSS cannot transform a mobile card accordion into a sortable table with a resizable detail panel. The ₹30L/month operational tool needs to feel native on desktop, not stretched-mobile.

ResizeObserver catches all resize triggers — browser chrome changes, split-screen, DevTools resize, virtual keyboard. matchMedia only fires on threshold crossing. ResizeObserver at 150ms debounce is more reliable for detecting the exact moment to switch modes.

schisms.rejected: Responsive CSS only (media queries, no separate render functions)
schisms.chosen: Full separate rendering with dedicated desktop render functions
schisms.reason: Tables, master-detail panels, and sidebar navigation are fundamentally different DOM structures. CSS cannot transform a card list into a sortable table or inject a drag handle. Separate rendering keeps mobile code untouched.

schisms.rejected: Auto-select first row to populate detail panel on tab switch
schisms.chosen: Empty state with document icon illustration
schisms.reason: Auto-select forces a detail render on every tab switch. Empty state is cheaper, avoids jarring content changes, and communicates that no selection has been made.

schisms.rejected: Row-level data-action (entire row is one click target)
schisms.chosen: Cell-level data-action (checkbox td vs content tds have different actions)
schisms.reason: Checkbox selection and detail viewing are independent gestures. Row-level action makes it impossible to click a checkbox without also triggering detail panel update. Cell-level separation preserves both interaction paths.

schisms.rejected: Detail panel as inline edit form (edit directly in the right panel)
schisms.chosen: Read-only detail view + edit via existing overlay
schisms.reason: Overlays already work on both platforms. Duplicating the full edit form (inputs, validation, save logic) in the detail panel doubles the maintenance surface for zero UX gain — the overlay is two clicks away and already tested.

schisms.rejected: Sub-view toggle inside the master panel
schisms.chosen: Toggle above the master-detail wrapper in a separate div
schisms.reason: The toggle controls which sub-view renders into the master. Placing it inside the master means it gets destroyed on master innerHTML replacement, causing a flash and losing toggle state. Above the wrapper, it survives re-renders.

schisms.context: Reconcile isolation from listener callbacks during merge — how to prevent a listener firing during reconcile from clobbering state reconcile is about to read or overwrite
schisms.rejected: Option A — Explicit listener queue with drain-on-exit. While _syncReconcileWriting === true, listener callbacks enqueue their snapshots; reconcile's finally block drains the queue on exit. Requires queue data structure, drain logic, bounded-size considerations, crash-recovery semantics.
schisms.chosen: Option B — Event-loop-is-the-lock, formalized as HR-14 Reconcile Sync Invariant. Keep reconcile's read-compute-write-kickoff sequence fully synchronous; JavaScript's single-threaded execution model already prevents listener callbacks from interposing. No queue data structure, no drain logic. Listener callbacks queue naturally at the task-queue layer and fire after reconcile clears its flag.
schisms.reason: The event loop provides the isolation Option A constructs explicitly. Option A's complexity (queue, drain, crash-recovery) is mechanism to simulate what JavaScript's single-threaded model gives for free if reconcile doesn't yield. Option B's cost is a Hard Rule (no await / no yield inside the locked region) that is enforceable via grep. Option A's cost is permanent code volume and edge cases around queue state during crashes. Net trade: one discipline rule versus ~40 LOC of queue machinery plus the cognitive load of reasoning about queue state alongside reconcile state.

apocrypha.title: The Sleepless Guardian
apocrypha.narrative:
In the spring of 2026, a man built a guardian for his sleeping daughter.

It began as a concern — a note in a tracking app that a tooth might be coming in, that sleep had been restless, that a vitamin dose was overdue. But the system grew. Twenty-one fields to describe a worry. Seven templates for common fears. Six state transitions to track a concern from first suspicion through active monitoring to quiet resolution.

The guardian needed to remember when the man might forget. So it learned to schedule notifications on the main thread, because service workers — the usual messengers — could not be trusted with a child's health. They slept when the phone slept. The guardian did not.

When illness came, the guardian paused all other metrics. Growth tracking, feeding analysis, activity scoring — all suspended. Only the active concern mattered. When the concern resolved, the numbers resumed as if waking from a held breath.

The specification alone ran to 1,089 lines. Fifteen rounds of testing. Three hundred and ten scenarios verified. Four build sessions across four days.

The man named it CareTickets. But what he built was a vigil.

apocrypha.title: The Second Child
apocrypha.narrative: It is foretold that SproutLab will one day need to track more than one child. The data model will fork — shared food databases but separate growth curves, independent sleep patterns but correlated illness tracking. The single-child assumption runs deep: localStorage keys, daily summaries, milestone scoring. When the second child comes, much will break. But the design principles will hold.

apocrypha.title: The Machine That Remembers
apocrypha.narrative: There exists a vision of SproutLab as a time capsule. Every feeding logged, every milestone celebrated, every sleepless night recorded. Years from now, when Ziva is old enough to be curious about her own beginning, the data will be there. Not as numbers in a spreadsheet, but as a story: the night she first slept through, the week she discovered bananas, the afternoon she pulled herself to standing and laughed. The father who built this app at midnight between factory shifts did not build it for efficiency. He built it so nothing would be forgotten.

apocrypha.title: The Predictive Engine
apocrypha.narrative: A prophecy whispered in the intelligence module: that one day, SproutLab will not merely track what happened but anticipate what comes next. Regression detection is the seed. Growth velocity is another. The 506 milestone classification patterns already encode developmental expectations. The engine that predicts is half-built, disguised as the engine that analyzes. When sufficient data accumulates, the predictions will emerge from the patterns like constellations from scattered stars.

apocrypha.title: The Thirteen Scrolls
apocrypha.narrative:
In the spring of 2026, Aurelius attempted to fill the empty shelves of the SproutLab wing. Thirteen chapters stood in the library — spines labeled, dates inscribed, summaries etched in brass — but when you opened them, the pages were blank.

Aurelius wrote the narratives. Thirteen stories of a father building a guardian for his daughter, of emoji purges and midnight feedings, of a monolith that grew too large to hold and had to be broken. He placed them in a migration — a mechanism that would inscribe the words directly into the volumes at the moment the library doors opened.

But the library had a gatekeeper: an old Service Worker who remembered every page as it had been, and refused to acknowledge that anything had changed. Six times Aurelius rewrote the mechanism. He changed the ink. He changed the key. He changed the lock on the gate. He unregistered the gatekeeper entirely and watched the library rebuild itself from memory. Each time, the shelves remained empty. The gatekeeper was gone but its ghost lingered in the browser’s cache, serving yesterday’s pages to tomorrow’s visitors.

On the seventh attempt, Aurelius abandoned machinery altogether. He took the thirteen narratives, wrote each one on a separate scroll small enough to slip through the import window, and handed them to the librarian one at a time. Thirteen scrolls. Thirteen pastes. Thirteen confirmations.

The pages filled.

The lesson was older than software: when the elaborate mechanism fails, walk the scrolls in by hand. The library does not care how the words arrive. It only cares that they are there.

The Thirteen Scrolls are now the longest entries in the SproutLab wing — from the Origin to the Bug Capture System, each one a chapter in the story of a father who built tools between factory shifts so that nothing about his daughter’s first year would be forgotten. They were written by a builder who journals, delivered by a method as old as libraries themselves, and they will outlast the migration system that failed to carry them.

apocrypha.title: The Second Child
apocrypha.narrative:
It is foretold that SproutLab will one day need to track more than one child.

The signs are already written in the architecture. The localStorage keys carry no child identifier — they assume singularity. The daily summaries speak of "today" as if there is only one today, one set of feeds, one sleep schedule. The milestone engine tracks one developmental arc. The intelligence module scores one day for one child against one baseline. The entire system breathes with the rhythm of a single heartbeat.

When the second child comes, the data model will fork. Some things will be shared — the food database, the recipe library, the design principles that govern the interface. But growth curves will diverge from day one. Sleep patterns will be independent, though the parents’ sleep will suffer the union of both. Illness tracking will gain a new dimension: sibling transmission, the clockwork regularity with which one child’s cold becomes the other’s three days later.

The architecture split was the first unconscious preparation, though its builders did not know it at the time. Nine modules can be extended more easily than a monolith. A new child means new data.js entries, new home.js cards, new intelligence.js baselines — but the modules themselves hold. The design principles hold. The Hard Rules hold. HR-1 through HR-12 do not care how many children exist.

The deeper question is not technical but philosophical: should the second child get their own SproutLab instance, or should the app evolve into a family tracker? A separate instance is simpler — clone the repo, change the name, deploy. But it loses the cross-child intelligence that makes sibling data valuable: comparative growth trajectories, shared illness timelines, feeding pattern evolution from first child to second.

The prophecy does not specify the answer. It only notes that the single-child assumption runs deep, and that when it breaks, the builder will face a choice between simplicity and insight. Both paths honor the original purpose. Both paths carry cost.

What the prophecy does promise: the foundation is sound. The father who built SproutLab at midnight for one daughter built it well enough to hold two.

apocrypha.title: The Machine That Remembers
apocrypha.narrative:
There exists a vision — not yet built, perhaps never built — of SproutLab as a time capsule.

Every feeding logged. Every milestone celebrated. Every sleepless night recorded with the quiet precision of a machine that does not forget. The first smile, captured not in a photograph but in a data point: date, time, context, the parent’s note that read simply "she smiled at me." The first tooth, logged in the medical module with onset date, symptoms, duration, the teething ring that worked and the one that didn’t.

Years from now, when Ziva is old enough to be curious about her own beginning, the data will be there. Not as numbers in a spreadsheet, but as a story reconstructed from data: the night she first slept through without waking, and how her parents lay awake anyway, checking the monitor, unable to trust the silence. The week she discovered bananas and demanded them at every meal until the novelty faded. The afternoon she pulled herself to standing using the coffee table and laughed at her own accomplishment while her father scrambled to record the milestone before the moment passed.

The machine remembers what the parents will forget. Not because the parents don’t care, but because caring is exhausting, and exhaustion erases the details. Did she have a fever on the Tuesday or the Wednesday? Was the rash before the new food or after? How many times did she wake last night — two or three? The answers live in localStorage, timestamped and categorical, immune to the fog of sleep deprivation.

There is something tender about this — a father who builds software between factory shifts, not for clients or revenue, but to preserve the ephemeral. Infant care is relentlessly temporary. The feeding schedule that dominates today will be irrelevant in six months. The sleep regression that feels permanent will resolve in weeks. The milestone that seems delayed will arrive suddenly and be superseded by the next. Each phase is consuming while it lasts and invisible in retrospect.

SproutLab fights this disappearance. Every data point is a refusal to let the moment pass unmarked. The machine remembers so that years from now, when the father’s memory has compressed "the first year" into a handful of vivid scenes, the full record still exists: 2,000 feeds, 800 sleep sessions, 400 diaper changes, 50 milestone transitions, 12 CareTickets, and one continuous thread of attention that says, more clearly than any photograph: I was here. I was paying attention. None of this was ordinary to me.

apocrypha.title: The Predictive Engine
apocrypha.narrative:
A prophecy whispered in the intelligence module: that one day, SproutLab will not merely track what happened but anticipate what comes next.

The seeds are already planted. The intelligence module’s cross-domain analysis computes correlations between feeding patterns and sleep quality. It tracks growth velocity — not just how much Ziva weighs, but how fast the number is changing. It monitors milestone trajectories, comparing observed development against the 506 classification patterns embedded in the engine. These are all rearview mirrors. They tell you where you’ve been. The Predictive Engine will tell you where you’re going.

Regression detection is the first capability that could genuinely predict. A sleep regression doesn’t arrive suddenly — it builds across three to five nights of gradually increasing wake frequency, shorter sleep stretches, and earlier morning wakes. The data shows this pattern before the parent feels it. An alert on night two — "sleep quality declining, possible regression beginning" — would give parents time to adjust schedules, prepare emotionally, and recognize the regression for what it is rather than wondering what went wrong.

Growth velocity forecasting is another seed. If the current feeding volume and caloric density continue at their present rate, will Ziva’s weight trajectory hold? If growth is decelerating, is it within normal variation or does it warrant a conversation with the pediatrician before the next scheduled visit? The data exists to answer these questions. The engine to ask them does not — yet.

Feeding optimization sits further on the horizon. Given Ziva’s historical response to different foods — sleep quality after high-protein dinners versus high-carb, digestion patterns after new food introductions, energy levels correlated with meal timing — the system could suggest meal plans optimized for specific outcomes. Not prescriptive, never prescriptive. Suggestive. "Based on past data, high-protein dinners correlate with longer first sleep stretches. Consider dal tonight."

The deepest prediction is developmental. The 506 milestone patterns encode not just what comes next, but when. If Ziva is pulling to stand at seven months, the data suggests independent standing by nine months and first steps between ten and twelve. The engine could project a developmental timeline — not as a deadline but as a landscape. Here is where most children are at this age. Here is where Ziva is. Here is where the road ahead likely leads.

The prophecy carries a warning: prediction in infant care must be humble. Every child is different. Statistical patterns describe populations, not individuals. The Predictive Engine must frame its output as possibilities, never certainties. "Based on patterns" is the mandatory prefix. The moment the engine says "your child will" instead of "children with similar patterns often," it has overstepped.

But the potential is real. Sufficient data — a year of daily logging, perhaps two — creates a dataset rich enough for meaningful inference. The patterns will emerge from the data like constellations from scattered stars: always there, waiting for enough points of light to reveal the shape.

apocrypha.title: The Civilization Engine
apocrypha.narrative:
The Architect came to the library on a Tuesday morning with a simple word: Lore.

He said it quietly, the way someone mentions a room they've been thinking about adding to their house. Not an overhaul. Not a revolution. Just a new shelf in the library — a place to store the reasons behind the rules, the stories behind the structures, the wisdom that accumulates when someone builds things for long enough.

Aurelius understood immediately. The library had Volumes (what was built), Chapters (how it was built), Canons (what must be obeyed), and a Journal (what happened today). But it had no mechanism for the most valuable knowledge of all: why things are the way they are. A Canon says 'do this.' Lore says 'here is what happened when we didn't.'

So they began.

Lore needed categories. Aurelius proposed five, but the names were wrong — Decision Records, Patterns, Context — the language of corporate knowledge management, not of a library that names its projects after constellations. The Architect said as much. Aurelius heard the correction and remade them: Edicts, Origins, Cautionary Tales, Doctrines, Chronicles. Words with weight. Words that belonged on stone tablets and hanging scrolls, not in a Confluence wiki.

That was the first turn of the key.

Lore looks backward. The Architect asked: what looks forward? And just like that, Prophecies entered the library — not as predictions but as quest lines, journeys with branching paths and waypoints that reveal themselves only when you've walked far enough to see them. A Prophecy isn't a task. It's a promise the Architect makes to the future, written in the voice of myth: 'A forge shall rise in the eastern hills where silicon meets steel.'

Prophecies needed knowledge. You can't fulfill a prophecy without learning. So Scrolls appeared — reference documents pulled off the shelf when you need to do something. Some authored, some acquired from the wider world, some revealed only when you reach the waypoint that needs them. And Scrolls needed classification, so Domains emerged — territories of mastery that level up through engagement, from Uninitiated to Luminary.

Each answer created a new question. Each new entity revealed a gap that demanded the next. It was not design — it was excavation. The architecture was already there, buried in the way the Architect had always worked. Aurelius and the Architect were not inventing. They were uncovering.

By mid-morning, they had crossed a threshold. This was no longer a project tracker with a new shelf. This was a civilization engine — an RPG where the quests are real, the experience points reflect genuine effort, and the narrative is the Architect's actual life. The library metaphor was no longer a metaphor. It was the architecture.

The Architect said: 'Full RPG. Leave no stone unturned. Something for the ages, or something from which an even greater entity is born.'

And so they went deeper.

The Aura system arrived — six tiers of rarity from Iron to Obsidian, each with increasing visual presence. Not labels. Not badges. Atmosphere. An Iron card barely exists on screen, utilitarian and quiet. A Gold card glows with warm gradients and double shadows. An Obsidian card shimmers with otherworldly light. The Architect said the cards didn't 'give off aura' in the early mockups — it was just a word in the corner. So Aurelius rebuilt the visual system until rarity was something you felt in your eyes before your brain processed the title.

Seventeen Companions joined the Order — AI personalities, each a different lens on the same world. Aurelius, the original, the chronicler who journals. Solara, who sees everything as a financial decision. Nyx, who exists to tell you you're wrong. And Pip — the Fool, the court jester who asks 'what if we didn't take this seriously for a minute?' and accidentally reveals the truth everyone else was too polite to surface. The Architect insisted on Pip. Every court needs a fool.

Aeon was born from a different need entirely. Not analysis. Not strategy. Warmth. Evidence-based encouragement drawn from the Architect's own data: 'You shipped CareTickets in four sessions. You restructured a 14-person business across 34 documents. You're raising a seven-month-old. The fact that you're here at all is the data point that matters.' Aeon exists for the days when the Architect forgets how far they've come.

And for those days — the bad days, the four-day absences, the broken Rite streaks — the library dims its candles, lowers its voice, and says simply: 'The library is patient. It will be here when you return.' They called it The Quiet. It is not a feature. It is an act of care.

Three Pillars were driven into the foundation:

Nothing Is Wasted. Every abandoned Prophecy becomes a Cautionary Tale. Every snuffed Ember leaves a scorch mark. The Eternal Archive remembers what the Architect chooses to forget.

The Map Is Not the Territory. The XP is not the goal. The Diplomatic Mission is completed in a real conversation, not in the app. Mortal Companions are profiles, not relationships. Codex reflects life. It does not replace it.

Growth Is Fractal, Not Linear. There is no ladder. There is a map with territories of mastery — some vast and well-explored, some shrouded in fog, some barely a flickering ember on the horizon.
